---
title: "Ch 12a — Limits"
subtitle: "What value does f(x) approach as x sneaks up on a — even when f(a) is undefined?"
lesson-id: "cbse11.limits"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 12
ncert-chapter-title: "Limits and Derivatives"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.relations-and-functions", "cbse11.trig-functions"]
unlocks: ["cbse11.derivatives-intro", "cbse12.continuity"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute $f(2)$ when $f(x) = x^2 + 1$ in your head?
2. Are you fluent with factoring $x^2 - 9 = (x - 3)(x + 3)$?
3. Do you know $\sin\theta / \theta$ tends to a limit as $\theta \to 0$
   (even informally)?
4. Can you sketch $y = 1/x$ and explain what happens near $x = 0$?

Three confident yeses — read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a calculator. Enter $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$ and try to
compute $f(2)$. The calculator complains: division by zero. But try
$x = 1.9, 1.99, 1.999, \ldots$ and $x = 2.1, 2.01, 2.001, \ldots$.

| $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|:--|:--|
| 1.9 | 3.9 |
| 1.99 | 3.99 |
| 1.999 | 3.999 |
| 2.001 | 4.001 |
| 2.01 | 4.01 |
| 2.1 | 4.1 |

The values converge to **4** from both sides. We say "the limit of $f$
as $x$ approaches $2$ is $4$" — even though $f(2)$ itself is not
defined. The widget below lets you watch the squeeze.

```{ojs}
viewof xt = Inputs.range([1.5, 2.5], {value: 2.001, step: 0.0001, label: "x (avoid x = 2)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const x = xt;
  if (Math.abs(x - 2) < 1e-10) {
    return html`<div style="color:#a00;">f(2) is undefined (division by zero).</div>`;
  }
  const f = (x * x - 4) / (x - 2);
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>x = ${x.toFixed(4)}</b></div>
    <div><b>f(x) = (x² − 4)/(x − 2) = ${f.toFixed(6)}</b></div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      Slide x toward 2. The values approach 4 — but x never quite arrives.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

A limit is "the destination", not "the value at the destination".

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. The function $f(x) = (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2)$ simplifies to $x + 2$ for
   $x \ne 2$. So why didn't we just say "plug in $x = 2$, get $4$"?
2. What does it mean for $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$ to equal $1$?
3. Is it possible for a function to *not* have a limit at a point?
4. What if the left-hand limit and the right-hand limit at $x = a$
   disagree?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) is the reason limits are *worth* defining: you can extract the
value the function "wants to be" at a hole. (4) is exactly the
**jump discontinuity** that makes $\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ fail to exist.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Direct substitution

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 3} (x^2 + 2x - 1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Polynomials are continuous — just plug in.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$9 + 6 - 1 = 14$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — 0/0 by factoring

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Direct substitution gives $0/0$. Factor.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x - 1} = x + 1$ for $x \ne 1$.

Limit $= 1 + 1 = 2$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Rationalising

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + x} - 1}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply by the conjugate $\sqrt{1 + x} + 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{(\sqrt{1+x} - 1)(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)} =
\dfrac{1 + x - 1}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+x} + 1}$.

Limit at $x = 0$: $\dfrac{1}{2}$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Standard trig limit

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is one of the *standard limits* you must memorise (and which is
proved by a squeeze argument in radians).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1$.

Equivalently $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan x}{x} = 1$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Use the standard

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 5x}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Force the form $\sin(\text{stuff})/\text{same stuff}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\sin 5x}{x} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{\sin 5x}{5x}$.

As $x \to 0$, $5x \to 0$, so $\sin(5x)/(5x) \to 1$. Limit $= 5$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Limit at infinity

$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{3x^2 + 5x + 1}{x^2 - 7}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide top and bottom by the highest power of $x$ in the denominator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{3 + 5/x + 1/x^2}{1 - 7/x^2}$.

As $x \to \infty$, $5/x, 1/x^2, 7/x^2 \to 0$. Limit $= 3$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Left and right limits

Consider $f(x) = \dfrac{|x|}{x}$. Find $\lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x)$ and
$\lim_{x \to 0^-} f(x)$. Does $\lim_{x \to 0} f(x)$ exist?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For $x > 0$, $|x| = x$, so $f(x) = 1$. For $x < 0$, $|x| = -x$, so
$f(x) = -1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Right limit: $1$. Left limit: $-1$. They disagree, so the two-sided
limit **does not exist**.
</details>

You have just used **direct substitution**, **factoring**,
**rationalising**, **standard trig limits**, **limits at infinity**, and
**left/right limits**. Now we name them.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Limit (informal).** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L$ means: as
$x$ gets arbitrarily close to $a$ (but $x \ne a$), $f(x)$ gets
arbitrarily close to $L$.

**Two-sided limit exists** iff
$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = L$.

**Algebra of limits.** If both limits exist:
$$ \lim (f \pm g) = \lim f \pm \lim g, \quad \lim (fg) = (\lim f)(\lim g) $$
$$ \lim \dfrac{f}{g} = \dfrac{\lim f}{\lim g} \text{ (provided } \lim g \ne 0). $$

**Standard limits to memorise:**
$$ \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1, \quad \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan x}{x} = 1, \quad \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos x}{x^2} = \dfrac{1}{2}. $$
$$ \lim_{x \to a} \dfrac{x^n - a^n}{x - a} = n a^{n-1}. $$
$$ \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{e^x - 1}{x} = 1, \quad \lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\log(1 + x)}{x} = 1. $$

(Use only those listed in the rationalised NCERT — the trig and
$x^n - a^n$ are the headline three for boards.)

**Strategy menu** for $0/0$ form:

1. Factor and cancel.
2. Rationalise (for radicals).
3. Recognise a standard limit and force the form.

:::

A short verse:

> *Approach $a$ but never arrive;*
> *The value the function aims at is alive.*
> *If left and right disagree at the gate,*
> *No limit awaits — they cancel and break.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} \dfrac{x^3 - 8}{x^2 - 4}$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Substitute $x = 2$: $0/0$. Need to factor.
2. $x^3 - 8 = (x - 2)(x^2 + 2x + 4)$. $x^2 - 4 = (x - 2)(x + 2)$.
3. Cancel: $\dfrac{x^2 + 2x + 4}{x + 2}$.
4. Substitute: $\dfrac{4 + 4 + 4}{4} = 3$.

**Answer:** $3$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\lim_{x \to 1} (3x^2 - 5x + 2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 - 5 + 2 = 0$.
</details>

**W2.** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan 3x}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 \cdot \tan(3x)/(3x) \to 3$.
</details>

**W3.** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{2x + 1}{5x - 3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Divide by $x$: $(2 + 1/x)/(5 - 3/x) \to 2/5$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** $\lim_{x \to 2} \dfrac{x^4 - 16}{x - 2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use $\lim (x^n - a^n)/(x - a) = n a^{n-1}$ with $n = 4, a = 2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 \cdot 2^3 = 32$.
</details>

**P2.** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x - x \cos x}{x^3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use Taylor expansions of $\sin x = x - x^3/6 + \cdots$ and $\cos x = 1 - x^2/2 + \cdots$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin x \approx x - x^3/6$. $x \cos x \approx x - x^3/2$.

Numerator: $-x^3/6 + x^3/2 = x^3/3$.

Limit: $1/3$.
</details>

**P3.** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\cos x - 1}{x^2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Standard limit: $(1 - \cos x)/x^2 \to 1/2$. Negate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-1/2$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + x} - \sqrt{1 - x}}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Rationalise.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Multiply by $\dfrac{\sqrt{1+x} + \sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x} + \sqrt{1-x}}$:

$\dfrac{(1+x) - (1-x)}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + \sqrt{1-x})} = \dfrac{2x}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + \sqrt{1-x})} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{1+x} + \sqrt{1-x}}$.

At $x = 0$: $2/2 = 1$.
</details>

**S2.** $\lim_{x \to \pi/4} \dfrac{\sin x - \cos x}{x - \pi/4}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At $x = \pi/4$, top is $0$ and bottom is $0$. Substitute $h = x - \pi/4$
and use compound-angle identities.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $h = x - \pi/4$. Then $\sin x = \sin(\pi/4 + h) = (\sin h + \cos h)/\sqrt{2}$,
$\cos x = (\cos h - \sin h)/\sqrt{2}$.

$\sin x - \cos x = \dfrac{2 \sin h}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \sin h$.

So the limit becomes $\lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{\sqrt{2} \sin h}{h} = \sqrt{2}$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 4x}{2x}$ equals
(a) $1$ (b) $2$ (c) $4$ (d) $1/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\sin 4x}{2x} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\sin 4x}{4x} \to 2$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{x^2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 - \cos 2x = 2 \sin^2 x$. So $\dfrac{2 \sin^2 x}{x^2} \to 2$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{x^{15} - 1}{x^{10} - 1}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use $\lim (x^n - 1)/(x - 1) = n$. Divide top by bottom by $(x - 1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x^{15} - 1}{x^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{(x^{15} - 1)/(x - 1)}{(x^{10} - 1)/(x - 1)}$.

Limit numerator: $15$. Limit denominator: $10$. Ratio: $15/10 = 3/2$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Evaluate (i) $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan 2x - x}{3x - \sin x}$,
(ii) $\lim_{x \to a} \dfrac{\sin x - \sin a}{x - a}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For (i), expand using $\tan x \approx x$ and $\sin x \approx x$ at
leading order.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\dfrac{\tan 2x - x}{3x - \sin x} = \dfrac{x(2\sec^2(c) - 1)}{x(3 - \cos(c'))}$
(by mean-value-like approximation), or more concretely:

$\tan 2x - x = (\tan 2x)/x \cdot x - x \to (2 - 1)x = x$ at leading order
($\tan 2x / (2x) \to 1$, so $\tan 2x \to 2x$ at leading order, giving
$\tan 2x - x \to x$).

$3x - \sin x \to 3x - x = 2x$ at leading order.

Limit: $1/2$.

**(ii)** Use sum-to-product: $\sin x - \sin a = 2 \cos\dfrac{x + a}{2} \sin\dfrac{x - a}{2}$.

$\lim \dfrac{2 \cos\frac{x+a}{2} \sin\frac{x-a}{2}}{x - a} = \cos a \cdot \lim \dfrac{2 \sin\frac{x - a}{2}}{x - a} = \cos a \cdot 1 = \cos a$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 12 LA on standard trig limits
matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the substitution trick (Stretch S2) is JEE Main
standard.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\lim f(x) = f(a)$ always."** Only when $f$ is continuous at $a$.
   Many Class 11 problems are crafted with $0/0$ holes precisely to
   force you to compute limits *not* by direct substitution.
2. **"$\sin x / x = 1$ at $x = 0$."** No — $\sin 0 / 0$ is undefined.
   The *limit* is $1$.
3. **"Limit at infinity is computed by plugging in $\infty$."** Treat
   limits at infinity by *dividing* by the highest power, not by
   substituting a number.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state the four standard limits for boards from memory?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you spot when a limit needs factoring, rationalising, or a
   standard-limit force-fit?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Derivatives (Introduction)](../12b-derivatives-intro/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** drill the three strategies (factor / rationalise /
  standard-limit) on five fresh limits.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Relations and Functions](../02-relations-and-functions/index.qmd)** —
  the limit is a property of a function.
- **[Trigonometric Functions](../03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)** —
  $\sin x/x \to 1$ requires radians.

### What this unlocks

- **[Derivatives (Introduction)](../12b-derivatives-intro/index.qmd)** —
  the derivative *is* a particular limit.
- **[Class 12 — Continuity & Differentiability](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/04-continuity/index.qmd)**.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 12 — Limits and Derivatives** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 12.1–12.3: limits, algebra of limits,
  standard trigonometric limits.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t4.limits`](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/limits/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Calculus.** Every theorem in calculus rests on the limit
concept. The intermediate value theorem, the mean value theorem, the
fundamental theorem of calculus — all start with limits.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Numerical optimisation studies "what does the loss
function approach as the parameters approach the optimum?" — exactly a
limit question.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (limits as the value the function "wants to
be"), Better Explained (the destination metaphor), Russian school
(Discover sequence forces the four canonical patterns), Bhāskara
(verse).
:::
